questions.title: Definition of an Algorithm
questions.description: Which of the following is the best definition of an algorithm?
options: Any program written in C or Python
A finite sequence of well-defined steps that solves a class of problems
A procedure that always uses recursion
A heuristic that produces approximate answers
questions.explanation: An algorithm is a finite, well-defined sequence of steps that, when applied to a valid input, terminates and produces a correct output for the problem it solves. It is a language- and machine-independent concept; the C/Python program is just one implementation.
hints: Focus on the abstract definition, not on a specific programming language
Key properties: finite, well-defined, deterministic, terminating

questions.title: Partial vs. Total Correctness
questions.description: An algorithm is said to be totally correct if:
options: It produces the correct output whenever it terminates
It always terminates for valid inputs and produces the correct output
It uses no more than O(1) extra memory
questions.explanation: Total correctness = partial correctness + termination. Partial correctness alone only requires that the algorithm produces the right output IF it terminates. Total correctness additionally requires that the algorithm always terminates for every valid input.
hints: Partial correctness is a conditional statement (IF it terminates...)
Total correctness adds a guarantee of termination

questions.title: Loop Invariant
questions.description: What is a loop invariant?
options: A value that never changes during the loop
A condition that holds before the loop, after every iteration, and after the loop terminates
A guarantee that the loop terminates
A bound on the number of iterations
questions.explanation: A loop invariant is a logical statement (a property of the program state) that is true before the loop starts, is preserved by every iteration, and is therefore true after the loop terminates. Together with the termination condition it lets us conclude what the loop establishes.
hints: It is a logical condition, not a single variable
Three things: initialisation, maintenance, termination

questions.title: Loop Invariant for Sum
questions.description: Consider the loop: s = 0; for i = 1 to n: s = s + i. Which is a correct loop invariant just before the test of iteration i (i.e., when i is about to take the value k)?
questions.explanation: Just before iteration k runs, the previous iterations have added 1, 2, ..., k-1. So the invariant is s = 1 + 2 + ... + (k-1) = (k-1)k/2. After iteration k completes we then have s = 1 + 2 + ... + k.
hints: Think about which values have already been added when iteration k is about to start
The invariant should be true at the moment the loop test is evaluated

questions.title: Big-O Definition
questions.description: f(n) = O(g(n)) means that there exist positive constants c and n0 such that for all n >= n0:
questions.explanation: Big-O is an asymptotic upper bound. f(n) = O(g(n)) iff there exist c > 0 and n0 such that 0 <= f(n) <= c*g(n) for all n >= n0. It says g grows at least as fast as f, ignoring constants and small inputs.
hints: Big-O is an upper bound
We only care about behaviour for large n

questions.title: Big-Omega Definition
questions.description: f(n) = Omega(g(n)) means that there exist positive constants c and n0 such that for all n >= n0:
questions.explanation: Big-Omega is an asymptotic lower bound. f(n) = Omega(g(n)) iff there exist c > 0 and n0 such that f(n) >= c*g(n) for all n >= n0. It says g grows at most as fast as f.
hints: Omega is the dual of O (lower vs upper)
Flip the inequality compared to Big-O

questions.title: Big-Theta Definition
questions.description: f(n) = Theta(g(n)) is equivalent to:
questions.explanation: Big-Theta is a tight bound: f(n) = Theta(g(n)) iff f is both O(g(n)) and Omega(g(n)). Equivalently, there exist constants c1, c2 > 0 and n0 with c1*g(n) <= f(n) <= c2*g(n) for all n >= n0.
hints: Theta = both upper and lower bound
The two functions grow at the same rate

questions.title: Asymptotic Ordering
questions.description: Order the following functions from slowest growing to fastest growing as n -> infinity: log n, n, n log n, n^2, 2^n.
options: log n < n < n log n < n^2 < 2^n
n < log n < n log n < n^2 < 2^n
log n < n log n < n < n^2 < 2^n
log n < n < n^2 < n log n < 2^n
questions.explanation: The standard asymptotic ordering is: 1 < log n < sqrt(n) < n < n log n < n^2 < n^3 < 2^n < n!. So the correct ascending order here is log n < n < n log n < n^2 < 2^n.
hints: log n grows slower than any positive power of n
Exponential beats every polynomial

questions.title: Big-O of a Polynomial
questions.description: Which is the tightest correct bound for f(n) = 5n^3 + 100 n^2 + 17?
questions.explanation: For a polynomial of degree d, the tight bound is Theta(n^d), so it is also O(n^d). The dominant term 5n^3 determines the growth rate. O(n^4) and O(2^n) are correct but not tight; O(n^2) is incorrect (lower order).
hints: Constants and lower-order terms drop out
Look at the highest power of n

questions.title: Logarithm Base in Big-O
questions.description: True or false: O(log_2 n) and O(log_10 n) describe the same set of functions.
options: True, because log_a n and log_b n differ only by a constant factor
False, because base-2 logarithms grow faster
False, because base-10 logarithms grow faster
True, but only when n is a power of 10
questions.explanation: log_a n = log_b n / log_b a, so logarithms in different bases differ only by a multiplicative constant. Big-O ignores constant factors, so O(log_2 n) = O(log_10 n) = O(log n). That is why we usually write 'log n' without specifying the base in complexity analysis.
hints: Use the change-of-base formula
Big-O hides constant factors

questions.title: Linear Search Worst Case
questions.description: What is the worst-case running time of linear search in an unsorted array of n elements?
questions.explanation: Linear search may have to inspect every one of the n elements (when the target is the last one or absent), giving worst-case time O(n).
hints: What if the element is not present?
Each element is inspected at most once

questions.title: Binary Search Requirement
questions.description: Binary search has worst-case running time O(log n), but only if:
options: The array length is a power of two
questions.explanation: Binary search relies on dividing the search interval in half at each step using the order of the elements. It therefore requires the array to be sorted. Duplicates, length, and element type do not affect correctness.
hints: How does binary search decide which half to recurse into?
Without order, halving the array tells you nothing

questions.title: Insertion Sort Complexity
questions.description: What are the best-case and worst-case running times of insertion sort?
options: Best O(n log n), worst O(n^2)
Best O(n log n), worst O(n log n)
questions.explanation: Insertion sort runs in O(n) on an already-sorted array (each element is compared once and never shifted) and O(n^2) on a reverse-sorted array (each new element must be shifted past all previous ones).
hints: Think about an already sorted input
Think about a reverse sorted input

questions.title: Merge Sort Complexity
questions.description: What is the worst-case running time of merge sort on an array of size n?
questions.explanation: Merge sort splits the array in half (log n levels of recursion) and merges in linear time at each level, giving Theta(n log n) in best, average, and worst case.
hints: It uses divide and conquer
Recurrence: T(n) = 2 T(n/2) + n

questions.title: Quicksort Worst Case
questions.description: What is the worst-case running time of quicksort, and when does it occur?
options: O(n^2), when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element
O(n^2), when the input is random
O(n), when the input is sorted
questions.explanation: Quicksort runs in O(n^2) in the worst case, which arises when the pivot at each step is the minimum or maximum (e.g., choosing the first element as pivot on an already-sorted input). The recurrence becomes T(n) = T(n-1) + n. With random pivots the expected time is O(n log n).
hints: What recurrence does an extremely unbalanced split produce?
Sorted input with first-element pivot is the classic bad case

questions.title: Stable Sorting
questions.description: Which of the following sorting algorithms is NOT stable in its standard implementation?
questions.explanation: A sort is stable if equal elements keep their relative order. Insertion, merge, and bubble sort are stable. Standard in-place quicksort is NOT stable because the partition step can swap equal elements past each other.
hints: Stability concerns equal keys, not the running time
In-place partitioning is the troublemaker

questions.title: Selection Sort Comparisons
questions.description: Selection sort on an array of n elements performs how many comparisons in the worst case?
questions.explanation: Selection sort makes (n-1) + (n-2) + ... + 1 = n(n-1)/2 comparisons regardless of the input, since it always scans the unsorted suffix to find the minimum.
hints: Number of comparisons does not depend on the input order

questions.title: Recurrence for Merge Sort
questions.description: Which recurrence describes the worst-case running time of merge sort?
questions.explanation: Merge sort splits the input into two halves (two recursive calls of size n/2) and combines them with a linear-time merge, giving T(n) = 2 T(n/2) + n. By the Master Theorem this solves to Theta(n log n).
hints: Two subproblems, each of half the size
The merge step is linear in n

questions.title: Master Theorem Application
questions.description: By the Master Theorem, what is the asymptotic solution to T(n) = 2 T(n/2) + n^2?
questions.explanation: Here a = 2, b = 2, so n^{log_b a} = n^1 = n. The driving function f(n) = n^2 grows polynomially faster than n (case 3 of the Master Theorem), and the regularity condition holds. Therefore T(n) = Theta(f(n)) = Theta(n^2).
hints: Compute n^{log_b a} = n^{log_2 2} = n
Compare f(n) = n^2 to n^1

questions.title: Recurrence: Halving
questions.description: What is the asymptotic solution to T(n) = T(n/2) + 1, with T(1) = 1?
questions.explanation: Each step halves n and does O(1) work, giving log_2 n levels of recursion. By the Master Theorem with a=1, b=2, f(n)=1, n^{log_b a} = 1, case 2 applies and T(n) = Theta(log n). This is the recurrence for binary search.
hints: How many times can you halve n before reaching 1?
This is the classic binary search recurrence

questions.title: Greedy Algorithm Idea
questions.description: Which best describes a greedy algorithm?
options: It explores all possibilities and returns the global optimum
It makes the locally optimal choice at each step, hoping to find a global optimum
It always solves problems in O(log n)
questions.explanation: A greedy algorithm builds up a solution piece by piece, always choosing the option that looks best at the current step (the locally optimal choice). For some problems (e.g., minimum spanning tree, activity selection) this provably yields a global optimum; for others it does not.
hints: Greedy = best-looking choice right now
It does not backtrack

questions.title: Greedy: Activity Selection
questions.description: For the activity-selection problem (choose the maximum number of non-overlapping activities), which greedy criterion is provably optimal?
options: Pick the activity with the earliest start time
Pick the activity with the shortest duration
Pick the activity with the earliest finish time
Pick the activity with the latest start time
questions.explanation: Selecting the compatible activity that finishes earliest leaves the resource free as soon as possible for the next activity, and an exchange argument shows this strategy is optimal. The other criteria can produce sub-optimal selections on simple counter-examples.
hints: Which criterion leaves the most room for future activities?
Earliest start and shortest duration both have classic counter-examples

questions.title: Divide and Conquer Steps
questions.description: The classical divide-and-conquer paradigm has three steps. Which list is correct?
questions.explanation: Divide-and-conquer: (1) DIVIDE the problem into smaller subproblems of the same form; (2) CONQUER the subproblems by solving them recursively (or directly when small enough); (3) COMBINE the subproblem solutions into a solution for the original problem.
hints: Three D's and C's
Merge sort follows exactly this template

questions.title: Counting Steps in a Loop
questions.description: A nested loop runs: for i = 1..n: for j = 1..i: do O(1) work. What is the total running time?
questions.explanation: The inner loop runs i times, so the total work is sum_{i=1..n} i = n(n+1)/2 = Theta(n^2).
hints: Use the closed form n(n+1)/2

questions.title: Properties of a Loop Invariant
questions.description: Which of the following must a loop invariant satisfy in order to prove a loop correct? (Select all that apply)
options: Initialisation: it holds before the first iteration
Maintenance: if it holds before an iteration, it holds before the next
Termination: when the loop ends, it (together with the exit condition) implies the desired postcondition
It must be expressible as a single arithmetic equation
questions.explanation: A loop invariant proof has three parts: Initialisation (the invariant is true before the loop starts), Maintenance (each iteration preserves it), and Termination (when the loop ends, the invariant plus the negated guard gives the postcondition). The invariant can be any logical predicate; it does not have to be a single equation.
hints: Three I/M/T parts
The invariant is a predicate, not necessarily a formula

questions.title: Algorithms Running in Theta(n log n)
questions.description: Which of the following sorting algorithms run in Theta(n log n) on every input (worst case)? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Merge sort and heapsort have worst-case running time Theta(n log n). Quicksort with first-element pivot is O(n^2) on already-sorted input, and insertion sort is O(n^2) in the worst case (reverse-sorted).
hints: Worst-case, not average-case
Heapsort uses a binary heap to repeatedly extract the maximum

questions.title: Recurrence: Linear Recursion
questions.description: What is the asymptotic solution to T(n) = T(n-1) + n with T(0) = 0?
questions.explanation: Unfolding the recurrence: T(n) = n + (n-1) + (n-2) + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2 = Theta(n^2). This is the recurrence describing quicksort's worst case and selection sort's running time.
hints: Unfold the recurrence and recognise an arithmetic sum

questions.title: Master Theorem: Case 1
questions.description: By the Master Theorem, what is the solution to T(n) = 8 T(n/2) + n^2?
questions.explanation: a = 8, b = 2, so n^{log_b a} = n^{log_2 8} = n^3. f(n) = n^2 grows polynomially slower than n^3 (case 1), so T(n) = Theta(n^{log_b a}) = Theta(n^3).
hints: Case 1: f(n) = O(n^{log_b a - eps})

questions.title: Fill: Loop Invariant Proof Steps
questions.description: A proof using a loop invariant has three steps: {blank1}, {blank2}, and {blank3}.
questions.explanation: Initialisation: the invariant holds before the loop starts. Maintenance: each iteration preserves the invariant. Termination: when the loop exits, the invariant (together with the negated guard) implies the desired postcondition.
hints: Three steps starting with I, M, T
Same structure as induction (base case, inductive step)

questions.title: Fill: Asymptotic Notation Names
questions.description: f(n) = {blank1}(g(n)) means g is an asymptotic upper bound; f(n) = {blank2}(g(n)) means g is an asymptotic lower bound; f(n) = {blank3}(g(n)) means f and g grow at the same rate.
questions.explanation: Big-O is an upper bound, Big-Omega is a lower bound, Big-Theta is a tight bound (both upper and lower).
hints: O for upper, Omega for lower, Theta for tight
Theta = O AND Omega

questions.title: Average Case vs Worst Case
questions.description: Which statement about quicksort is correct?
options: Worst case Theta(n log n), average case Theta(n log n)
Worst case Theta(n^2), average case Theta(n log n)
Worst case Theta(n^2), average case Theta(n^2)
Worst case Theta(n log n), average case Theta(n)
questions.explanation: Quicksort has worst-case running time Theta(n^2) (e.g. already-sorted input with naive pivot) but its expected (average) running time on a random permutation is Theta(n log n), which is also why it is fast in practice.
hints: Different inputs give different running times
Average is over random inputs / random pivot choices

questions.title: Lower Bound for Comparison Sorts
questions.description: Any comparison-based sorting algorithm requires, in the worst case, at least how many comparisons to sort n elements?
questions.explanation: A decision-tree argument shows that any comparison-based sort must distinguish n! permutations, requiring a tree of depth at least log_2(n!) = Omega(n log n). Algorithms like merge sort and heapsort achieve this bound.
hints: Think of the decision tree of comparisons
log(n!) = Theta(n log n) by Stirling's approximation

questions.title: Termination Argument
questions.description: To prove that a loop terminates, we typically:
options: Exhibit an integer-valued quantity that strictly decreases each iteration and is bounded below
Show that the loop body has constant time
Show that the invariant holds after the loop
questions.explanation: A standard termination argument exhibits a 'variant' or 'measure': a non-negative integer expression that strictly decreases on each iteration. Since a strictly decreasing sequence of non-negative integers must reach 0, the loop must terminate.
hints: Look for a quantity that gets smaller each iteration
Integers cannot decrease forever if bounded below

questions.title: Best vs Worst Case for Insertion Sort
questions.description: Why is the best case of insertion sort O(n)?
options: Because the array is already sorted, so each new element only needs one comparison and no shifts
Because the algorithm uses divide and conquer when the array is sorted
Because sorting always takes linear time
Because there are no comparisons in the best case
questions.explanation: If the array is already sorted, the inner while-loop of insertion sort exits immediately after one comparison, performing no shifts. The outer loop runs n-1 times with O(1) work each, giving total O(n).
hints: What does the inner loop do on a sorted array?
Each new element is already in the correct position

questions.title: Total Number of Operations
questions.description: An algorithm performs T(n) = 3 n^2 + 5 n + 7 operations on an input of size n. Which statements are true?
questions.explanation: Polynomials of degree d are Theta(n^d). Here d = 2, so T(n) = Theta(n^2). It is also O(n^2), Omega(n^2), and O(n^3), but those are not tight; Theta is the tight description.
hints: Drop constants and lower-order terms
Theta is the tight bound
